competency_cluster: cluster_01: Marriage, Civil Partnership, and Family Structures
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what they know about different types of relationships and their legal status.; Teacher introduces the three relationship types and students discuss what legal differences might exist.; Students work in groups to research and identify specific rights and protections for each relationship type.; Class creates shared understanding of legal distinctions through guided discussion.; Students help refine language for each rubric level based on their learning.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What different types of legal relationships do you know about?; What legal rights or protections might be different between these relationship types?; How would you explain these differences to someone who doesn't know about them?; What would it look like to go beyond just explaining the basics?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that clearly show progression from basic identification to complete explanations with accurate legal distinctions.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain legal relationship differences.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some relationship types but I confuse their legal distinctions.
student_rubric_developing: I can name relationship types but my explanations of legal differences are incomplete or inaccurate.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain the legal differences between marriage, civil partnership, and cohabitation including their respective rights and protections.
student_rubric_extending: I can compare legal frameworks across jurisdictions or analyse implications for specific scenarios.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I clearly explain what makes each relationship type legally different?; Have I included the specific rights and protections for each type?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple comparison chart showing the three relationship types.; Ask the student to identify which type of relationship they are most familiar with.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help the student focus on one clear legal difference between two relationship types.; Provide specific examples of rights that differ between relationship types.
feedback_moves_developing: Guide the student to check their explanations against reliable legal sources.; Ask the student to identify which rights and protections they may have missed.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge the student to consider how these laws might differ in other countries.; Encourage the student to apply their knowledge to real-world scenarios or case studies.

competency_cluster: cluster_01: Marriage, Civil Partnership, and Family Structures
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm ways families can change over time through discussion and personal reflection.; Students identify what makes parenting successful by examining different parenting scenarios.; Students connect parenting characteristics to early childhood development through guided exploration.; Students organize their ideas into performance levels from basic to advanced understanding.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are different ways families might change as time goes on?; What do you think makes someone a successful parent?; How do parenting choices affect young children's growth and development?; What would it look like to understand these topics really well versus just starting to learn?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select examples that show clear differences between basic family change descriptions and detailed parenting-development connections across various family structures.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet described how families change or identified parenting characteristics.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some family changes or parenting features with help but my understanding is incomplete.
student_rubric_developing: I can describe basic family changes and some parenting characteristics but miss connections to development.
student_rubric_competent: I can describe how families change over time and identify successful parenting characteristics including early childhood development connections.
student_rubric_extending: I can analyze complex family transitions and evaluate parenting approaches across diverse family structures and developmental stages.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain both how families change AND what makes parenting successful?; Have I connected parenting characteristics to early childhood development?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide sentence starters about family changes or parenting qualities.; Use visual prompts showing different family situations to spark initial thinking.
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide students to elaborate on their initial ideas with specific examples.; Ask clarifying questions to help students express their thinking more completely.
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt students to connect their parenting ideas to how children grow and develop.; Encourage students to add more detail about the family changes they described.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge students to consider how parenting might differ across various family types.; Ask students to analyze more complex family transitions beyond basic changes.

competency_cluster: cluster_03: Respectful Relationships and Self-Worth
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what equality means in relationships and daily life; Discuss legal rights and responsibilities students already know; Explore connections between feeling good about yourself and healthy relationships; Create success criteria for explaining both concepts clearly
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does it mean when people are treated equally?; How does feeling confident about yourself affect your relationships?; What would a strong explanation of equality and self-worth include?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work that shows clear progression from basic identification of concepts to sophisticated connections between equality, self-esteem, and relationship health.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain equality rights or self-esteem
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some equality concepts with help but my explanations need work
student_rubric_developing: I can explain basic equality rights on my own but struggle to connect self-esteem to healthy relationships
student_rubric_competent: I can independently explain legal equality rights and responsibilities and describe how self-esteem supports healthy relationships
student_rubric_extending: I can connect equality principles and self-worth to broader relationship dynamics and personal development strategies
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain both equality rights and self-esteem clearly without help?; Have I shown how self-esteem connects to healthy relationships?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide sentence starters about equality or self-esteem; Offer visual organizers to structure thinking about rights and relationships
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide students to complete partial explanations with specific prompts; Provide examples of clear explanations to model structure
feedback_moves_developing: Ask probing questions about how self-esteem affects relationship choices; Provide scenarios to practice connecting concepts
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge students to consider broader applications of equality and self-worth; Encourage exploration of personal development strategies

competency_cluster: cluster_03: Respectful Relationships and Self-Worth
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what respectful communication looks and sounds like in daily interactions.; Students identify specific challenges that make respectful communication harder during conflicts.; Students develop criteria for what makes communication respectful versus disrespectful.; Students create levels from basic attempts to advanced application of respectful communication strategies.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does respectful communication look like when you disagree with someone?; How do you know if your communication is being respectful or not?; What makes respectful communication harder in some situations than others?; How could someone who is really good at this help others improve?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear differences between respectful and disrespectful communication across various relationship contexts and conflict intensities.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to use respectful communication strategies.
student_rubric_emerging: I can try basic respectful communication with help but am not consistent.
student_rubric_developing: I can communicate respectfully in familiar situations but struggle during conflicts.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently use communication strategies that show respect in relationships and conflicts.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply respectful communication in new situations and help others develop these skills.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I use words and tone that showed respect for the other person?; How well did I handle disagreement or conflict while staying respectful?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Model specific respectful communication phrases and have student practice repeating them.; Provide sentence starters for respectful responses in common situations.
feedback_moves_emerging: Point out moments when student used respectful communication successfully.; Practice respectful communication strategies through role-play with immediate feedback.
feedback_moves_developing: Help student identify what makes conflict situations more challenging for respectful communication.; Practice applying familiar respectful communication strategies to conflict scenarios.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to use respectful communication in unfamiliar or complex social situations.; Have student reflect on how they could teach respectful communication strategies to others.

competency_cluster: cluster_04: Addressing Bullying and Ending Relationships
coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences or observations of different behaviors to build a shared understanding.; Students categorize behaviors into bullying and non-bullying groups with teacher facilitation.; Students discuss the effects of bullying on individuals and communities.; Students brainstorm and organize help-seeking options from informal to formal support.; Students review and refine the complete rubric levels together.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What behaviors have you seen that made someone feel unsafe or upset repeatedly?; How do different types of bullying affect people differently?; Who are the trusted adults and resources you could turn to for help?; What would quality work look like at each level of understanding?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select scenarios that clearly demonstrate different bullying types and varying levels of student response quality. Choose examples that show progression from basic identification to complex situation analysis.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify bullying or describe how to get help.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some types of bullying with support but my explanations need improvement.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify basic bullying types and their impact but need help with comprehensive help-seeking strategies.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify different types of bullying, explain their impact accurately, and describe appropriate ways to get help.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply bullying recognition skills to complex situations and evaluate multiple intervention strategies effectively.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I clearly explain what makes something bullying versus conflict or teasing?; Do I know multiple specific people and resources I could contact for help?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of bullying scenarios to analyze together.; Guide student through identifying one clear type of bullying behavior.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask student to explain their thinking about why certain behaviors count as bullying.; Provide specific examples to help student distinguish between bullying types.
feedback_moves_developing: Challenge student to identify additional help-seeking resources beyond their initial responses.; Ask student to explain the connection between bullying type and appropriate intervention.
feedback_moves_competent: Present complex scenarios that require applying bullying concepts to new situations.; Ask student to evaluate the effectiveness of different intervention approaches.

competency_cluster: cluster_05: Stereotypes, Prejudice, and Power Dynamics
coconstruction_stages: Students share examples of stereotypes they've encountered or heard about.; Class discusses what makes stereotypes harmful and how they affect people.; Students explore how power differences make stereotypes more damaging.; Groups identify how stereotypes can make harmful behaviors seem normal or acceptable.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What stereotypes have you noticed about different groups of people?; How might having more power make someone's stereotypes more harmful to others?; When do stereotypes make unfair treatment seem normal or okay?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from basic stereotype identification to sophisticated analysis of power dynamics and systemic harm.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain stereotypes or power dynamics.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some stereotypes with help but my explanations are incomplete.
student_rubric_developing: I can explain basic stereotype concepts but struggle to connect power inequalities to relationship damage.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain how stereotypes and power inequalities damage relationships and normalise harmful behaviours.
student_rubric_extending: I can analyse complex interactions between stereotypes, power dynamics, and systemic relationship harms across different contexts.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I clearly explain what stereotypes are and how they harm relationships?; Have I connected power differences to how stereotypes cause damage?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of common stereotypes to build foundational understanding.; Use guided questions to help identify stereotypes in familiar contexts.
feedback_moves_emerging: Support with sentence starters to structure explanations about stereotype impacts.; Provide specific examples showing how stereotypes affect relationships.
feedback_moves_developing: Guide connections between power differences and stereotype harm through questioning.; Use scenarios to demonstrate how power makes stereotypes more damaging.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge to examine stereotype impacts across different social contexts.; Prompt analysis of how systems maintain harmful stereotypes and power imbalances.

competency_cluster: cluster_06: Online Safety and Privacy
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what good behaviour looks like in different offline settings; Students identify similar expectations in online spaces they use; Students compare and find connections between online and offline behavioural expectations; Students create explanations for why expectations should be consistent across contexts
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does respectful behaviour look like in our classroom versus at home?; How do these same expectations apply when we communicate online?; Why should our behaviour standards be the same whether we're face-to-face or digital?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work that shows clear progression from basic rule identification to sophisticated explanations of consistent expectations across contexts.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain online behavioural expectations.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some basic online rules with help but my explanations need work.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify online behavioural expectations on my own but struggle to connect them with offline behaviour.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain how behavioural expectations apply consistently in both online and offline situations with clear reasoning.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my understanding to new digital situations and explain complex applications of consistent behavioural expectations.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why the same behaviour rules apply both online and offline?; Do my explanations include clear reasoning and connections between different contexts?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide examples of basic online rules to get started; Ask what good behaviour looks like in familiar offline settings
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide student to elaborate on incomplete explanations; Provide sentence starters for connecting online rules to reasons
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt student to compare specific online and offline situations; Ask how offline expectations might apply to digital interactions
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to consider new or unfamiliar digital platforms; Ask how behavioural expectations might apply in complex online scenarios

competency_cluster: cluster_07: Sharing Images and Legal Consequences
lt_name: Understanding Legal Risks of Image Sharing
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what students already know about online image sharing risks; Discuss what makes something a 'legal consequence' versus other types of consequences; Categorize different types of legal risks and their severity; Refine understanding of what makes an explanation 'comprehensive' versus 'incomplete'
coconstruction_student_prompts: What risks do you think exist when people share intimate images online?; How would you know if your explanation covers all the important legal consequences?; What would make one explanation more complete than another?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student explanations that show clear differences in accuracy and completeness of legal consequences identified.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain legal consequences of image sharing
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some risks with help but my explanations need work
student_rubric_developing: I can identify legal risks on my own but my explanations need more detail
student_rubric_competent: I can accurately and completely explain the legal consequences and serious risks of sharing intimate images online
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my understanding to analyze complex scenarios or connect to broader legal frameworks
student_rubric_self_checks: Have I explained both the legal consequences and the serious risks?; Is my explanation accurate and complete enough that someone else would understand the full picture?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple scenario to help student begin thinking about consequences; Ask what they think might happen legally when intimate images are shared
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide student to verify accuracy of their identified risks; Help student add missing legal consequences they haven't considered
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt student to elaborate on consequences they've identified; Ask student to check if they've covered all major legal risks
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to apply their understanding to a new complex scenario; Encourage student to explore connections to broader legal principles

competency_cluster: cluster_07: Sharing Images and Legal Consequences
coconstruction_stages: Students share what they already know about image sharing concerns and potential problems; Class brainstorms what makes a response appropriate versus inappropriate when seeing concerning material; Students discuss who they could report to and what information would be needed; Groups create action steps for different scenarios and share with class; Class refines understanding of when and how to help others with similar concerns
coconstruction_student_prompts: What concerns might you have about images being shared online or in messages?; Who could you talk to if you saw something that worried you?; What would make one response better than another in these situations?; How would you know if your action plan was working?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that represent clear progression from simple recognition to complex decision-making across different contexts and severity levels.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown I can identify appropriate responses to image sharing concerns
student_rubric_emerging: I can recognise some concerns with help but my suggested responses need improvement
student_rubric_developing: I can identify image sharing concerns on my own but sometimes miss important actions or reporting steps
student_rubric_competent: I can determine appropriate actions and identify correct reporting procedures when concerned about circulated material
student_rubric_extending: I can apply response strategies to new situations and help others understand appropriate actions and reporting procedures
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain what actions I would take and who I would report to?; Would my response help solve the problem and keep people safe?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Guide student to identify one specific concern about shared images; Provide sentence starters for describing what they notice
feedback_moves_emerging: Help student evaluate why their suggested response might not work; Model thinking through consequences of different actions
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt student to identify missing steps in their action plan; Ask student to consider who else might need to be involved
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to apply their approach to a different type of scenario; Ask student to explain their reasoning to help teach others

competency_cluster: cluster_08: Deepfakes, Harmful Content, and Online Harms
coconstruction_stages: Introduce the concept of deepfakes and harmful content through discussion of what students already know.; Examine obvious examples together to identify clear warning signs and red flags.; Explore more subtle examples to develop criteria for sophisticated detection.; Create shared language for different levels of identification skills.; Finalize rubric levels based on student input and examples discussed.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What makes you suspicious that online content might be fake or harmful?; How can you tell the difference between obvious fakes and more convincing ones?; What techniques do you use when you're unsure if content is authentic?; How would you describe someone who is really skilled at spotting deepfakes?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select a range of deepfake and harmful content examples from obviously manipulated to highly sophisticated, ensuring examples represent different manipulation techniques and content types.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify deepfakes or harmful content.
student_rubric_emerging: I can recognise some obvious fake content with support but miss subtle manipulations.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently identify clear deepfakes and harmful content but struggle with sophisticated examples.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify deepfakes and recognise various forms of harmful online content including illegal material and distorted representations.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify sophisticated deepfakes and analyse manipulation techniques used in harmful content creation.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I spot warning signs in content that others might miss?; Do I understand the different techniques used to create fake or harmful content?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide guided practice with obviously fake examples.; Teach basic warning signs to look for in suspicious content.
feedback_moves_emerging: Practice with moderately difficult examples without support.; Focus on developing independence in applying detection strategies.
feedback_moves_developing: Expose to sophisticated deepfakes and manipulation techniques.; Teach advanced detection methods and technical indicators.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge with cutting-edge deepfake technology examples.; Develop skills in analysing creation methods and manipulation techniques.

competency_cluster: cluster_08: Deepfakes, Harmful Content, and Online Harms
coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences of feeling unsure or worried online without naming specific incidents.; Class brainstorms who they might turn to when something online feels wrong or scary.; Students categorize different types of online concerns and match them to appropriate support sources.; Class creates criteria for what makes a support source trustworthy and accessible.
coconstruction_student_prompts: When have you felt confused or uncomfortable about something you saw online?; Who are the trusted adults or resources you could talk to about online problems?; What makes someone a good person to ask for help with online issues?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that represent common online concerns students face while avoiding graphic details or triggering content.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet identified any sources of support for online concerns.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some people who might help with online problems but my choices may not always be the best ones.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify several appropriate people or resources to help with online concerns but sometimes miss important options.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify appropriate sources of advice and support when encountering concerning online content or experiences.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my support-seeking skills to new online situations and help others find appropriate support.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name at least three different trusted sources I could turn to for help with online problems?; Do I know when to choose different types of support for different online concerns?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Guide student to think of one trusted adult they could talk to about problems.; Provide a simple list of school or community support resources to review together.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help student evaluate why certain support choices might be more helpful than others.; Practice matching different types of online concerns to appropriate support sources.
feedback_moves_developing: Encourage student to consider additional support options they may have overlooked.; Discuss when different contexts might require different types of support.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to consider how their support-seeking approach might work in unfamiliar online situations.; Invite student to help a peer think through their own support options.

competency_cluster: cluster_09: Online Scams, Data Privacy, and Emerging Technology Risks
lt_name: Evaluating and Responding to Digital Threats
coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences with suspicious messages or apps they've encountered.; Class brainstorms what makes something seem trustworthy or risky online.; Students examine examples of scams and new technologies to identify warning signs.; Groups create criteria for evaluating digital threats at different skill levels.; Class refines criteria into a shared rubric for threat evaluation.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What red flags help you spot something suspicious online?; How do you decide if a new app or website is safe to use?; What would you tell a friend who received a suspicious message?; How might someone who's really good at this help others stay safe?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from obvious scams to subtle threats, including both familiar and emerging technologies that students can relate to.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to evaluate digital threats or scams.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify obvious scams with help but struggle to evaluate new technology risks.
student_rubric_developing: I can recognise common scams and basic technology risks but miss subtle threats.
student_rubric_competent: I can evaluate new technologies for risks and respond appropriately to various scams.
student_rubric_extending: I can anticipate risks in unfamiliar technologies and help others respond to threats.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why something online seems risky or safe?; Would I know how to respond if I encountered a new type of scam?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Start by examining one clear example of an obvious scam together.; Practice identifying one simple warning sign in digital messages.
feedback_moves_emerging: Compare obvious scams with more subtle ones to build pattern recognition.; Introduce basic questions for evaluating new apps or websites.
feedback_moves_developing: Analyze examples where initial impressions might be misleading.; Practice applying evaluation criteria to unfamiliar technologies or platforms.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge thinking with novel scenarios that require transferring known principles.; Encourage explaining threat evaluation strategies to help others learn.

competency_cluster: cluster_10: Consent, Boundaries, and Pressure in Relationships
coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences of feeling pressured in relationships without naming specific people; Class brainstorms what pressure looks like and feels like in different relationship contexts; Students discuss what healthy responses to pressure might look like; Groups identify what it means to avoid putting pressure on others; Class agrees on criteria for recognising and managing relationship pressure effectively
coconstruction_student_prompts: When have you felt like someone wanted you to do something you weren't sure about?; What are the signs that someone might be feeling pressured by us?; How can we respond when we feel pressured while still maintaining good relationships?; What does it look like when someone handles pressure really well?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that represent common relationship pressures students face while ensuring examples show clear differences between recognising obvious versus subtle pressure.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown that I can identify or respond to relationship pressure
student_rubric_emerging: I can recognise some pressure with help but my responses are not always appropriate
student_rubric_developing: I can identify clear pressure situations on my own but struggle with subtle pressure or knowing how to respond
student_rubric_competent: I can identify and resist different types of relationship pressure while avoiding pressuring others
student_rubric_extending: I can use pressure management skills in new situations and help others recognise relationship pressure
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I tell when someone is trying to pressure me and respond appropriately?; Do I avoid putting pressure on others in my relationships?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Help student identify one clear example of relationship pressure from their experience; Practice recognising pressure signals through role-play scenarios
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide student to evaluate whether their response maintained the relationship appropriately; Provide sentence starters for declining pressure respectfully
feedback_moves_developing: Present scenarios with subtle pressure and ask student to identify the pressure signals; Practice multiple response strategies for complex pressure situations
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to apply their skills in unfamiliar relationship contexts; Ask student to coach a peer through a pressure situation

competency_cluster: cluster_10: Consent, Boundaries, and Pressure in Relationships
coconstruction_stages: Begin by asking students what they think 'consent' means in relationships and friendships.; Guide students to brainstorm what else might be important in caring relationships beyond just saying 'yes' or 'no'.; Have students discuss scenarios where consent exists but additional care considerations matter.; Support students in organizing their ideas into levels of understanding about care beyond consent.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does it mean to truly care for someone in a relationship or friendship?; Can you think of a time when someone had permission to do something but it still wasn't the kindest choice?; What other things should we consider besides just getting consent when we want to care for others?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear distinctions between basic consent and deeper care considerations like emotional wellbeing and respect.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain care beyond consent.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify that relationships involve more than consent with support.
student_rubric_developing: I can explain that care involves more than consent with some reasoning.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain that kindness and care requires considering feelings, wellbeing, and respect beyond basic consent.
student_rubric_extending: I can explain care beyond consent and connect this principle to broader ethical relationship frameworks.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain what care looks like beyond just getting permission?; Have I included examples of how feelings and wellbeing matter in caring relationships?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide sentence starters about what care means in relationships.; Ask the student to think about how they like to be treated by friends.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help the student identify specific examples of care beyond permission.; Guide them to explain their thinking more clearly with prompting questions.
feedback_moves_developing: Encourage the student to provide more detailed reasoning for their examples.; Ask them to consider multiple aspects of care like feelings and wellbeing.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge the student to connect their understanding to broader relationship principles.; Ask them to consider how this applies across different types of relationships.

competency_cluster: cluster_11: Personal Safety and Recognising Unsafe Relationships
coconstruction_stages: Students share what makes them feel safe or unsafe with people.; Class identifies patterns in trustworthy versus untrustworthy behaviors.; Students discuss how to recognize warning signs in different relationship types.; Groups create reasoning strategies for evaluating safety in complex situations.; Class refines criteria for helping others recognize safety indicators.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What behaviors make you trust someone more or less?; How do you know when a relationship feels safe?; What would you look for in a tricky situation where you're not sure about someone?; How could you help a friend recognize if someone might not be trustworthy?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from obvious red flags to subtle warning signs across different relationship contexts like friendships, online interactions, and authority figures.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown that I can evaluate whether people and relationships are trustworthy and safe.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some obvious safety concerns with help but my reasoning needs work.
student_rubric_developing: I can recognize clear safety issues on my own but struggle with subtle warning signs or complex situations.
student_rubric_competent: I can evaluate trustworthiness and relationship safety using good reasoning strategies across different relationship contexts.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my evaluation skills to new situations and help others recognize trustworthiness and safety indicators.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain my reasoning for why I think someone is or isn't trustworthy?; Would I be able to help a friend recognize warning signs in their relationships?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Guide student to identify one obvious safety concern in a clear example.; Ask student to describe what makes them feel safe with someone they trust.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help student practice explaining their reasoning for safety concerns.; Provide structured questions to guide their evaluation process.
feedback_moves_developing: Point out subtle warning signs the student missed in practice scenarios.; Challenge student with more complex relationship situations to analyze.
feedback_moves_competent: Present novel contexts for the student to apply their evaluation skills.; Ask student to coach a peer through recognizing safety indicators.

competency_cluster: cluster_11: Personal Safety and Recognising Unsafe Relationships
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm situations where students might need help or need to report harmful behaviour; Identify different types of help sources available in various contexts; Match appropriate help sources to specific harmful situations; Discuss when and how to escalate concerns through proper reporting procedures
coconstruction_student_prompts: What situations might require you to seek help or report something harmful?; Who are the trusted adults or resources you could turn to in different settings?; How do you decide which help source is most appropriate for a specific situation?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select examples that show clear distinctions between informal help-seeking and formal reporting procedures across different severity levels of harmful behaviour.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet identified ways to seek help or report concerns about harmful behaviour
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some people who might help me but need support understanding when and how to report concerns
student_rubric_developing: I can identify basic help sources but sometimes struggle to choose the right response for specific harmful situations
student_rubric_competent: I can identify appropriate ways to seek help and report concerns about harmful behaviour when needed
student_rubric_extending: I can apply help-seeking knowledge to new situations and help others understand appropriate reporting procedures
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I identify who to contact for different types of harmful situations?; Do I understand the difference between seeking help and making formal reports?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of help-seeking situations students might encounter; Introduce basic vocabulary around help sources and reporting procedures
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide students to distinguish between different types of help sources; Practice matching help sources to specific situations with scaffolding
feedback_moves_developing: Provide scenarios to practice selecting appropriate responses to harmful behaviour; Discuss criteria for choosing between informal help and formal reporting
feedback_moves_competent: Present novel or complex situations requiring help-seeking decisions; Encourage students to explain their reasoning to support others' learning

competency_cluster: cluster_12: Sexual Harassment, Violence, and Abuse
coconstruction_stages: Introduce the learning target and discuss why clear definitions matter for safety and legal understanding.; Guide students to brainstorm different forms of harmful behaviors they may have heard about.; Facilitate research into legal definitions and help students organize behaviors into categories.; Support students in refining language to match legal terminology and identifying key distinguishing features.; Review the complete rubric levels and have students suggest anchor examples for each level.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What behaviors have you heard described as harassment or violence and why might legal definitions be important?; How can we organize these behaviors into clear categories that match legal frameworks?; What specific language do legal definitions use and how does this differ from everyday language?; What would it look like to apply these definitions to real situations?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select scenarios that clearly demonstrate each level of definitional accuracy and legal understanding without graphic content.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify sexual harassment or violence.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some forms with support but my definitions are incomplete or inaccurate.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently identify basic forms but struggle with legal definitions or exploitative behaviors.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify and describe different forms of sexual harassment, sexual violence, and exploitative behaviors including their legal definitions.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply definitions to analyze complex scenarios or connect legal frameworks across different contexts.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I accurately define each form using legal terminology?; Am I able to distinguish between different types of harmful behaviors and explain why the distinctions matter?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide basic vocabulary and definitions to begin building understanding.; Offer structured templates or graphic organizers to support initial identification attempts.
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide students to reliable legal sources to verify and correct their definitions.; Provide specific feedback on which parts of their definitions need refinement.
feedback_moves_developing: Direct students to legal resources that clarify exploitative behaviors and formal definitions.; Help students practice distinguishing between similar concepts using clear examples.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge students to apply their knowledge to analyze multi-layered scenarios.; Encourage exploration of how these definitions vary across different legal jurisdictions.

competency_cluster: cluster_12: Sexual Harassment, Violence, and Abuse
lt_name: Understanding Domestic Abuse Laws and Impact
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what they already know about domestic abuse and share initial understanding.; Teacher introduces the Domestic Abuse Act 2021 and students identify key provisions together.; Students discuss different types of victim impact and create categories.; Class collaboratively builds success criteria for explaining concepts, legislation, and impacts.; Students test the rubric against sample explanations and refine level descriptions.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does domestic abuse mean to you and what forms might it take?; What should good legislation include to protect victims?; How might domestic abuse affect different people in different ways?; What would make an explanation of these topics really clear and complete?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student explanations that clearly show progression from basic concept identification to detailed legislative knowledge to comprehensive impact analysis.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain domestic abuse concepts or legislation.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some domestic abuse concepts with support but my explanations need more accuracy.
student_rubric_developing: I can explain basic domestic abuse concepts and mention relevant legislation but need more detail on victim impact.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain domestic abuse concepts, describe the Domestic Abuse Act 2021 provisions, and identify impacts on victims including children.
student_rubric_extending: I can connect domestic abuse legislation to broader legal frameworks and analyse complex victim impact scenarios.
student_rubric_self_checks: Have I explained what domestic abuse is and included the key legal provisions?; Have I clearly described how domestic abuse affects victims including children?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple definition of domestic abuse and ask students to explain it in their own words.; Show a basic overview of the Domestic Abuse Act 2021 and ask what protection it might offer.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help students check their understanding against official definitions and correct any misconceptions.; Guide students to be more specific about which aspects of domestic abuse they are describing.
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt students to research specific provisions of the Domestic Abuse Act 2021 and add these details.; Ask students to consider how domestic abuse specifically affects children and other family members.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge students to research how domestic abuse legislation connects to other areas of law.; Encourage students to analyse more complex scenarios involving multiple types of victim impact.

competency_cluster: cluster_13: Harmful Practices and Physical Harms
coconstruction_stages: Introduce the learning target and discuss why understanding legal frameworks matters; Brainstorm what makes legal information accurate versus inaccurate; Explore what 'legal status and requirements' means in practice; Define what constitutes independent work versus needing support; Discuss how legal knowledge applies to complex real-world scenarios
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does it mean for something to be legally required or prohibited?; How can we tell if legal information is accurate and complete?; What would it look like to apply legal knowledge to a complicated situation?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select examples that clearly show the difference between accurate and inaccurate legal information, and include scenarios that demonstrate varying levels of independence in identifying legal requirements.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify legal requirements for harmful practices.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some harmful practices with support but my legal information contains inaccuracies.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently identify some harmful practices and their legal status with minor gaps.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently and accurately identify the legal status and requirements for FGM, strangulation, and related harmful practices.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my legal knowledge to analyse complex scenarios involving multiple harmful practices.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I accurately state what the law requires regarding these harmful practices?; Am I working independently or do I need support to identify legal information?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide structured prompts to help identify one specific harmful practice; Offer a simple legal framework template to begin with
feedback_moves_emerging: Check accuracy of legal information against reliable sources; Reduce support gradually while maintaining focus on one practice at a time
feedback_moves_developing: Point out specific gaps in legal knowledge to address; Encourage verification of legal information using multiple sources
feedback_moves_competent: Present complex scenarios involving multiple harmful practices; Challenge to explain how different legal requirements interact

competency_cluster: cluster_13: Harmful Practices and Physical Harms
coconstruction_stages: Introduce the learning target and explain why understanding physical dangers is important for health and safety.; Guide students to brainstorm what they already know about physical harm and safety risks.; Facilitate discussion about different levels of understanding from basic awareness to detailed knowledge.; Work together to define what complete and accurate descriptions of serious dangers would include.; Collaborate to establish criteria for extending understanding beyond basic descriptions.

coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select examples that clearly show progression from incomplete awareness to detailed understanding of serious health consequences and physiological impacts.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to describe physical dangers.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some dangers with help but my descriptions need more accuracy.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify physical dangers on my own but need to include more details about serious health consequences.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently describe the serious physical dangers associated with strangulation and harmful practices depicted in pornography.
student_rubric_extending: I can describe dangers and explain how they affect the body or connect to broader health implications.
student_rubric_self_checks: Have I included specific details about serious health consequences?; Can I explain my understanding without needing help or prompts?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide guided questions about basic safety and physical harm concepts.; Offer structured prompts to help identify one specific danger.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help clarify misconceptions through targeted questions about specific dangers.; Provide resources or examples to support more accurate descriptions.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask probing questions about the serious health consequences of identified dangers.; Guide toward including specific details about physical impacts and risks.
feedback_moves_competent: Encourage exploration of physiological mechanisms behind the dangers.; Prompt connections between specific dangers and broader health implications.

competency_cluster: cluster_14: Healthy Sexual Relationships and Consent
lt_name: Understanding Health Impacts of Sexual Choices

student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain how sexual choices affect health
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some health impacts with help but my explanations need more detail
student_rubric_developing: I can explain some health impacts of sexual choices but miss important connections
student_rubric_competent: I can explain how choices in sex and relationships positively or negatively affect all aspects of health and wellbeing
student_rubric_extending: I can analyze complex scenarios and connect multiple health dimensions comprehensively
student_rubric_self_checks: Have I considered physical, mental, emotional, and social health impacts?; Can I explain both positive and negative effects of different choices?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide sentence starters connecting choices to health outcomes; Use guided questions to help identify one simple health impact
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask clarifying questions to deepen incomplete explanations; Provide examples to model more accurate connections
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt consideration of missed health dimensions; Guide reflection on overlooked positive or negative impacts
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge with complex scenarios requiring deeper analysis; Encourage integration of multiple health dimensions simultaneously

competency_cluster: cluster_15: Contraception, Pregnancy Options, and Sexual Health
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what students already know about sexual health topics and prevention methods.; Discuss what makes sexual health information accurate versus incomplete or unclear.; Identify different levels of understanding from basic awareness to comprehensive knowledge.; Create criteria for what complete sexual health knowledge should include.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What sexual health topics do you think are important for people our age to understand?; How can you tell if sexual health information is complete and accurate?; What would it look like to have a basic understanding versus a thorough understanding of these topics?

student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to describe sexual health knowledge.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some contraceptive or STI prevention methods with help but may have some inaccuracies.
student_rubric_developing: I can describe some contraceptive options and STI prevention methods on my own but my understanding is not complete.
student_rubric_competent: I can accurately describe contraceptive options, pregnancy choices, STI prevention methods, and explain why testing is important.
student_rubric_extending: I can compare how effective different methods are and explain how personal circumstances affect health choices.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I accurately explain all four main areas: contraceptives, pregnancy choices, STI prevention, and testing importance?; Am I able to describe these topics without help and with correct information?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide basic vocabulary and definitions for sexual health terms.; Start with one specific area like contraceptive methods to build initial understanding.
feedback_moves_emerging: Check accuracy of current knowledge and correct misconceptions.; Provide reliable sources to verify and expand sexual health information.
feedback_moves_developing: Guide student to identify gaps in their current knowledge across all four topic areas.; Encourage research into areas they have not yet covered thoroughly.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to research effectiveness rates and compare different methods.; Ask student to consider how individual factors might influence health decisions.

competency_cluster: cluster_15: Contraception, Pregnancy Options, and Sexual Health
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what sexual health services exist in their community.; Students research and share where these services can be found locally.; Students discuss what makes information medically accurate and trustworthy.; Students organize findings into quality levels from basic to comprehensive.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What sexual health services do you think people might need?; Where in our community could someone find these services?; How can you tell if health information is accurate and reliable?; What would make someone really good at finding sexual health resources?

student_rubric_emerging: I can name some sexual health services with help but my location information needs work.
student_rubric_developing: I can find some sexual health services and locations on my own but miss important resources.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify where to access sexual health services, support, and accurate information from multiple sources.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify comprehensive sexual health resources and evaluate how accessible they are for different people.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name specific places where someone could get sexual health services?; Do I know how to find medically accurate information about sexual health?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a list of local sexual health service types to get started.; Guide student to identify one specific service location in their area.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help student verify location accuracy using official websites or directories.; Prompt student to identify additional service types they may have missed.
feedback_moves_developing: Challenge student to find services beyond the most obvious ones.; Guide student to identify multiple ways to access the same type of service.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask student to consider accessibility barriers for different populations.; Prompt student to evaluate the quality and reliability of their information sources.

competency_cluster: cluster_15: Contraception, Pregnancy Options, and Sexual Health
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm where they currently get health information and discuss what makes sources trustworthy.; Students examine sample sexual health sources and identify quality indicators together.; Students practice evaluating sources using agreed criteria and discuss how to share reliable information with peers.; Students refine evaluation criteria and create strategies for helping others find trustworthy sources.

student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to evaluate sexual health information sources.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some sexual health information sources with support but cannot evaluate their reliability.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently identify reliable sexual health sources but struggle to evaluate their quality or help others find good sources.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently evaluate sexual health information for reliability and effectively help others find trustworthy sources for decision-making.
student_rubric_extending: I can critically evaluate complex sexual health information and adapt my guidance strategies for different audiences.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why this sexual health source is reliable or unreliable?; Would I feel confident directing someone else to this source for important health decisions?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple checklist of source quality indicators to practice with.; Start with comparing two obviously different sources side-by-side.
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide practice in applying reliability criteria to specific sexual health sources.; Provide sentence starters for explaining why sources are trustworthy or not.
feedback_moves_developing: Practice explaining source quality to peers using clear reasoning.; Focus on developing simple language for signposting others to reliable sources.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge with complex or contradictory sources requiring deeper analysis.; Practice adapting signposting language for different audiences or situations.

feedback_moves_emerging: Model accurate emotion vocabulary in context; Guide students to connect feelings with specific situations
feedback_moves_developing: Introduce more precise emotion vocabulary; Help students identify specific wellbeing influences through questioning
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge students to explore emotional complexity; Encourage analysis of how multiple wellbeing factors interact

competency_cluster: cluster_16: Emotional Wellbeing and Mental Health Literacy

feedback_moves_emerging: Clarify specific misconceptions about normal versus concerning emotional experiences; Offer structured comparison frameworks
feedback_moves_developing: Present borderline cases with guided analysis; Help identify patterns that distinguish conditions from normal responses

competency_cluster: cluster_16: Emotional Wellbeing and Mental Health Literacy
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm activities that make them feel good and discuss why; Teacher guides students to group activities into participation, kindness, and barrier-overcoming categories; Students explore what barriers might prevent people from doing wellbeing activities; Class develops language for explaining how each strategy type contributes to mental wellbeing

feedback_moves_emerging: Ask students to explain why their identified activities might help someone feel better; Guide students to think about the mental benefits of their chosen activities
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt students to consider what might prevent someone from doing these activities; Help students make explicit connections between activity types and mental wellbeing
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge students to think about how these strategies might work in different contexts; Ask students to connect their understanding to broader mental health concepts

coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences or knowledge about online problems they've seen or heard about; Class brainstorms what makes some online situations harmful or concerning; Students discuss where they might go for help with different online problems; Groups match different types of online harms to appropriate reporting or support options; Class creates criteria for choosing the best help option for different situations
