feedback_moves_emerging: Help the student focus on the specific connection between weight and disease risk.; Provide clear examples of how extra weight can affect the body.
feedback_moves_developing: Guide the student to name the three specific diseases mentioned in the learning target.; Ask the student to explain what 'increased risk' means in their own words.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge the student to explain why weight affects these particular diseases.; Encourage the student to research additional weight-related health conditions.

competency_cluster: cluster_21: Infection Prevention, Dental Health, and Screening
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what they already know about preventing and treating infections.; Teacher guides students to organize ideas into categories of prevention methods, treatments, and resistance.; Students discuss what makes explanations complete versus incomplete for each category.; Class collaboratively defines what comprehensive understanding looks like at each level.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are all the ways we can prevent infections and treat them when they occur?; How would you know if someone really understands how antibiotics work versus just knowing they exist?; What would it look like to use infection knowledge to solve real-world health problems?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student explanations that clearly show progression from basic identification to detailed mechanisms to real-world application.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain how infections are prevented or treated.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some ways to prevent infections with help but my explanations need work.
student_rubric_developing: I can explain basic infection prevention and treatment on my own but need more detail on resistance and immunisation.
student_rubric_competent: I can accurately explain infection treatment, prevention, antibiotics, vaccination, immunisation and antimicrobial resistance.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply infection knowledge to evaluate health policies or solve new disease problems.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain how each prevention and treatment method actually works?; Could I use my infection knowledge to advise on a real health situation?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide basic infection examples and ask students to identify one prevention method.; Use visual aids showing infection transmission to spark initial thinking.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask students to explain why their identified prevention methods work.; Provide sentence starters to help structure more complete explanations.
feedback_moves_developing: Guide students to research specific mechanisms of immunisation and antimicrobial resistance.; Ask students to connect their basic knowledge to more complex processes.
feedback_moves_competent: Present novel disease scenarios and ask students to apply their comprehensive knowledge.; Challenge students to evaluate effectiveness of different prevention strategies.

competency_cluster: cluster_21: Infection Prevention, Dental Health, and Screening
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what health maintenance practices students already know about.; Sort practices into categories and identify which ones relate to dental and oral health.; Discuss what makes a good description versus a basic list of practices.; Explore connections between daily habits and long-term health outcomes.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What health practices do you already do to take care of yourself?; How would you explain to a younger student why these practices matter?; What's the difference between knowing what to do and understanding why it helps?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Look for student work that shows clear progression from listing practices to explaining benefits to making broader health connections.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet described any health maintenance practices.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some dental practices with help but my descriptions need work.
student_rubric_developing: I can describe basic dental practices on my own.
student_rubric_competent: I can describe dental health practices, explain why oral hygiene helps, and explain why regular check-ups matter.
student_rubric_extending: I can connect health maintenance practices to bigger health outcomes and prevention across different situations.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain not just what to do but why it matters for my health?; Have I included both daily practices and regular check-ups in my description?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple list of dental practices to start with.; Ask what the student already does to take care of their teeth.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help identify which descriptions are accurate and which need correction.; Provide sentence starters for clearer explanations.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask why each practice helps maintain health.; Prompt to include information about regular screenings and self-examination.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge to think about how these practices connect to overall health.; Ask how these practices might apply in different life situations or age groups.

competency_cluster: cluster_22: Sleep, Reproductive Health, and Accessing Healthcare
lt_name: Understanding Sleep Hygiene and Health Impacts
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what they know about good sleep habits and share experiences.; Teacher guides discussion to identify key sleep hygiene practices from student contributions.; Students explore connections between sleep and how they feel physically and mentally.; Class organizes ideas into levels of understanding from basic identification to detailed explanations.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What sleep habits help you feel rested and healthy?; How does poor sleep affect your body and mind?; What would someone need to explain to show they really understand sleep and health?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that show clear progression from listing sleep practices to explaining detailed connections between sleep hygiene and specific physical and mental health outcomes.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain sleep practices or health impacts.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some sleep practices with help but my explanations are incomplete.
student_rubric_developing: I can explain basic sleep hygiene practices but need more detail about health connections.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain sleep hygiene practices clearly and describe how sleep affects physical and mental wellbeing.
student_rubric_extending: I can connect sleep hygiene to broader health outcomes and evaluate individual sleep needs comprehensively.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain specific sleep practices and why they matter for health?; Do I describe connections between sleep and both physical and mental wellbeing?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple sleep habit checklist to begin identifying practices.; Ask student to share one thing they do before bedtime.
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide student to explain why one sleep practice helps health.; Provide sentence starters for connecting sleep habits to feeling better.
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt student to add specific examples of physical and mental health impacts.; Ask how sleep affects energy, mood, and thinking.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to consider how sleep needs vary by age or lifestyle.; Encourage exploration of long-term health consequences of sleep patterns.

competency_cluster: cluster_22: Sleep, Reproductive Health, and Accessing Healthcare
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what reproductive health means and list key terms students already know; Sort terms into categories like pre-conception care and pregnancy support; Define quality criteria for complete descriptions of reproductive health concepts; Test criteria against sample student responses to refine expectations
coconstruction_student_prompts: What comes to mind when you hear reproductive health?; How would you group these concepts into meaningful categories?; What makes a description of reproductive health complete and accurate?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that show clear progression from basic term identification to comprehensive descriptions with connections to broader health outcomes.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to describe reproductive health aspects.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some reproductive health terms with support but my descriptions are not accurate.
student_rubric_developing: I can describe basic reproductive health concepts but lack detail on pre-conception care or pregnancy support.
student_rubric_competent: I can describe key aspects of reproductive health including pre-conception care and pregnancy support accurately and comprehensively.
student_rubric_extending: I can connect reproductive health concepts to broader health outcomes and explain interrelated factors.
student_rubric_self_checks: Have I included both pre-conception care and pregnancy support in my description?; Can I explain how reproductive health connects to overall wellness?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide basic reproductive health vocabulary cards to build foundation; Start with guided discussion about what health means in general
feedback_moves_emerging: Correct misconceptions immediately with accurate information; Provide graphic organizers to structure thinking about reproductive health components
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt for specific details about pre-conception care practices; Ask for examples of pregnancy support services or interventions
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge to explain how reproductive health affects long-term family outcomes; Encourage exploration of social determinants that influence reproductive health

competency_cluster: cluster_23: Personal Safety, Risk Management, and Peer Influence
coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences where they felt unsafe or needed to make safety decisions; Groups brainstorm different types of risks they encounter in various environments; Class discusses what good risk management looks like and sounds like; Students create criteria for different levels of safety awareness and management skills
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are some situations where you've had to think about your safety?; How do you know when a situation might be risky?; What strategies help you stay safe in different places?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear differences in risk identification skills and management strategies across familiar and unfamiliar environments.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown that I can identify or manage safety risks
student_rubric_emerging: I can spot some obvious risks when someone helps me but need support to stay safe
student_rubric_developing: I can identify common safety risks in places I know well but find it hard to manage them on my own
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify and manage personal safety risks in different environments using good strategies
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my safety skills to new situations and help others become more safety aware
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I spot potential risks in this environment without help?; Do I have strategies ready to manage the risks I've identified?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Point out one obvious risk in the current environment and ask what they notice; Model simple safety thinking by narrating your own risk assessment process
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask them to identify one risk without your help first; Provide a choice between two safety strategies and discuss which might work better
feedback_moves_developing: Challenge them to identify risks in a less familiar environment; Have them practice explaining their safety strategy to someone else
feedback_moves_competent: Present a novel scenario and ask how they would adapt their safety approach; Ask them to mentor a peer who is still developing safety awareness

competency_cluster: cluster_23: Personal Safety, Risk Management, and Peer Influence
lt_name: Responding to Peer Influence and Conflict
coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences of peer pressure without naming specific people; Class identifies common situations where peer influence occurs; Students brainstorm appropriate and inappropriate responses to peer pressure; Groups develop strategies for handling conflict with peers; Class agrees on what effective peer influence management looks like
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does peer pressure feel like and when might it happen?; How can you tell if someone is trying to influence your decisions?; What are some ways to respond when peers pressure you or when conflicts arise?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that represent typical peer situations students face at their age level without identifying real individuals or creating embarrassing situations.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown that I can recognise or respond to peer influence and conflict.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some peer pressure situations with help but my responses need work.
student_rubric_developing: I can recognise obvious peer influence and conflict but find it hard to respond well in difficult situations.
student_rubric_competent: I can recognise peer influence and conflict situations and respond appropriately using effective strategies.
student_rubric_extending: I can handle complex peer situations and help others navigate influence and conflict effectively.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I identify when someone is trying to influence my decisions?; Do I have strategies that work well when dealing with peer pressure or conflict?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Help student identify one simple peer pressure scenario from their experience; Provide clear examples of what peer influence looks like in everyday situations
feedback_moves_emerging: Practice recognising peer pressure cues through role-play scenarios; Model appropriate response language and strategies for common situations
feedback_moves_developing: Rehearse responses to challenging peer situations through guided practice; Discuss why certain strategies work better in different contexts
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to apply skills in new or more complex peer scenarios; Encourage reflection on helping others with similar situations

competency_cluster: cluster_24: Weapons, Exploitation, and Seeking Help
coconstruction_stages: Introduce the concept of misconceptions and how they form about weapons; Explore together what students think they know about weapon prevalence; Examine how these beliefs might influence protection decisions; Define quality criteria for explaining the connection between misconceptions and protection beliefs
coconstruction_student_prompts: What do you think you know about how common weapons are and where did you learn this?; How might believing weapons are everywhere affect someone's choices about protection?; What would a clear explanation of this connection look like?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work that shows clear progression from identifying misconceptions to explaining their impact on protection beliefs.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to address weapon misconceptions
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some misconceptions with help but my explanations need work
student_rubric_developing: I can identify weapon misconceptions on my own but struggle to explain how they create false protection beliefs
student_rubric_competent: I can independently explain how misconceptions about weapon prevalence lead to false beliefs about needing protection
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my understanding to analyse how weapon misconceptions influence broader safety decision-making
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I clearly explain the connection between weapon misconceptions and protection beliefs?; Have I shown how false beliefs about weapon prevalence affect decision-making?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Start by identifying one common misconception about weapons; Think about where people get their ideas about weapon prevalence
feedback_moves_emerging: Focus on explaining why the misconception is incorrect; Connect the misconception directly to protection decisions
feedback_moves_developing: Strengthen the link between misconceptions and false protection beliefs; Use specific examples to show how misconceptions influence thinking
feedback_moves_competent: Consider how these misconceptions affect other safety decisions beyond immediate protection; Analyse the broader impact of weapon misconceptions on community safety choices

competency_cluster: cluster_25: Menstrual and Reproductive Health
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what they already know about menstrual health variations and concerns.; Teacher introduces key menstrual conditions and students sort them by familiarity.; Students examine condition descriptions and identify what makes a good characteristic description.; Class develops criteria for accurately identifying and describing menstrual health conditions.; Students test the rubric levels against sample condition descriptions.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What differences in menstrual experiences have you heard about or learned about?; Which of these conditions can you describe some characteristics for?; What makes this description of a condition clear and complete?; How would you know if someone accurately identified and described a menstrual condition?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select clear examples of condition descriptions at each level, focusing on accuracy and completeness of characteristics rather than complexity of conditions.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify menstrual health conditions.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some menstrual conditions with support but my descriptions of characteristics are incomplete or inaccurate.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently identify common menstrual conditions but my descriptions of characteristics have some gaps or inaccuracies.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify common menstrual health conditions and accurately describe their key characteristics.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify menstrual conditions and explain relationships between characteristics and underlying causes or treatments.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name the condition correctly and describe its main characteristics accurately?; Do my descriptions include the key features that distinguish this condition from others?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a list of common menstrual conditions to start with.; Offer guided practice identifying one condition with clear characteristics.
feedback_moves_emerging: Focus on one condition at a time and verify characteristics together.; Provide reference materials with accurate condition descriptions.
feedback_moves_developing: Point out specific gaps or inaccuracies in characteristic descriptions.; Have student compare their descriptions with reliable medical sources.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to explain why certain characteristics occur.; Ask student to connect characteristics to potential causes or treatment approaches.

competency_cluster: cluster_25: Menstrual and Reproductive Health
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what makes menstrual or reproductive health symptoms concerning versus normal.; Students discuss when they might need to talk to a healthcare provider about these concerns.; Students categorize different scenarios by urgency and type of support needed.; Students refine criteria for determining appropriate timing and level of healthcare support.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What symptoms would make you think 'this isn't normal'?; How do you decide if something needs immediate attention versus waiting?; What questions help you figure out if you need professional help?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that clearly show different levels of symptom severity and decision-making complexity about seeking healthcare support.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify when healthcare support might be needed.
student_rubric_emerging: I can recognize some concerning symptoms with help but cannot decide what action to take.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify obvious concerning symptoms but struggle to determine when professional healthcare support is needed.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently determine when menstrual and reproductive health concerns require healthcare support and identify appropriate timing for seeking help.
student_rubric_extending: I can evaluate complex symptom patterns and determine urgency levels for different healthcare support needs.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why certain symptoms are concerning and what action to take?; Do I know when to seek immediate versus routine healthcare support?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide examples of common concerning symptoms to build awareness.; Guide student through one simple scenario of determining healthcare needs.
feedback_moves_emerging: Help student practice identifying concerning symptoms independently.; Provide decision-making frameworks for determining appropriate actions.
feedback_moves_developing: Practice scenarios that require judgment about timing of healthcare support.; Discuss factors that influence when professional help is needed versus self-care.
feedback_moves_competent: Present complex multi-symptom scenarios requiring urgency assessment.; Challenge student to evaluate interconnected symptoms and their implications.

competency_cluster: cluster_25: Menstrual and Reproductive Health
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what students already know about fertility, menopause, and lifestyle factors affecting reproductive health; Discuss what makes a good explanation versus a poor explanation of these concepts; Sort student examples of explanations into quality levels from weakest to strongest; Refine level descriptions based on student input and add specific criteria for each level
coconstruction_student_prompts: What do you think makes an explanation of reproductive health clear and complete?; How would you know if someone really understands how lifestyle affects fertility and menopause?; What would be missing from a weak explanation versus a strong one?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose student explanations that show clear differences in accuracy, completeness, and depth of understanding about fertility, menopause, and lifestyle connections.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain reproductive health factors
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some fertility or menopause concepts with help but my explanations need work
student_rubric_developing: I can explain basic fertility and menopause concepts but need to add more about lifestyle impacts
student_rubric_competent: I can explain fertility, menopause, and how lifestyle factors impact reproductive health accurately and completely
student_rubric_extending: I can connect reproductive health factors to broader health outcomes and prevention strategies
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain all three areas: fertility, menopause, and lifestyle impacts clearly?; Are my explanations accurate and detailed enough for someone else to understand?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide basic definitions and examples of fertility and menopause; Use visual aids or diagrams to introduce reproductive health concepts
feedback_moves_emerging: Check understanding of key terms and correct misconceptions; Provide guided practice with accurate explanations
feedback_moves_developing: Focus on adding lifestyle factors and their specific impacts on reproductive health; Use examples to show connections between lifestyle choices and reproductive outcomes
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge to explore broader health connections and prevention strategies; Encourage analysis of how reproductive health fits into overall wellness

competency_cluster: cluster_26: First Aid and Emergency Response
coconstruction_stages: Introduce the importance of emergency response skills and why quality matters.; Have students brainstorm what makes emergency procedures effective versus ineffective.; Guide students to identify key components of proper CPR and first aid techniques.; Collaborate to define what different levels of skill look like from beginner to expert.; Finalize the rubric levels together and discuss how to use it for self-assessment.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What would you want someone helping you in an emergency to know and do well?; What are the most critical steps that cannot be skipped in CPR or first aid?; How can you tell the difference between someone who is still learning these skills versus someone who is confident and skilled?; What would it look like to go beyond basic procedures in a real emergency situation?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select video demonstrations or live examples that clearly show the contrast between hesitant, partially correct, fully competent, and adaptively skilled emergency response performances.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to demonstrate emergency response procedures.
student_rubric_emerging: I can attempt basic emergency procedures with significant help but my techniques need improvement for safety.
student_rubric_developing: I can demonstrate some emergency procedures on my own but I miss some important steps or techniques.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently demonstrate proper CPR techniques and basic first aid procedures accurately and safely.
student_rubric_extending: I can demonstrate emergency procedures fluently and adapt my techniques appropriately to different emergency scenarios.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I perform the procedures safely without guidance and in the correct sequence?; Would I feel confident using these skills in a real emergency situation?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide direct modeling of basic CPR hand positioning and compression technique.; Guide student through one simple first aid procedure step-by-step with full support.
feedback_moves_emerging: Focus on correcting one critical safety issue at a time rather than all errors simultaneously.; Provide verbal cues and physical guidance to improve technique accuracy.
feedback_moves_developing: Have student identify which steps they missed and practice those specific sequences.; Use checklists to help student self-monitor completeness of procedures.
feedback_moves_competent: Introduce scenario variations that require adapting standard procedures.; Challenge student to explain their decision-making process during procedures.
